# Connecting with Remote SSH {#sec-remote-ssh}

This chapter walks through connecting to Savio from VS Code or Positron using the Remote SSH extension. Once configured, you can develop interactively on a compute node just as you would on your local machine.

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure you have:

1. A BRC account with access to the Carleton group projects (see @sec-hpc-overview).
2. A PIN and a multifactor authentication (MFA) app (Duo, Authy, etc.) linked to BRC.
3. The [`lrc-scripts`](https://github.com/lbnl-science-it/lrc-scripts.git) repository cloned to your local machine.
4. VS Code or Positron with the **Remote - SSH** extension installed.

## One-time setup

### Step 1: Create an SSH certificate alias {#sec-cert-alias}

The `lrc-scripts` repo includes a helper script that requests a 12-hour SSH certificate from Savio. Creating a shell alias saves you from typing the full path each time.

Open your shell's run-commands file — `~/.zshrc` for Zsh on macOS, `~/.bashrc` for Bash — and add:

```bash
alias cert="~/path/to/lrc-scripts/request_cert.sh -p brc"
```

Update the path to wherever you cloned the repo. Restart your terminal, then run:

```bash
cert
```

You will be prompted for:

1. Your BRC username
2. Your 4-digit PIN (not the MFA code)
3. Your 6-digit MFA code from the authenticator app

If successful, you can now SSH into Savio without a password for the next 12 hours.

Test it:

```bash
ssh your_username@hpc.brc.berkeley.edu
```

### Step 2: Configure SSH hosts {#sec-ssh-config}

Edit (or create) `~/.ssh/config` and add the following entries. Replace `USER_NAME` with your BRC username.

```{.bash filename="~/.ssh/config"}
Host savio
    LogLevel QUIET
    User USER_NAME
    HostName hpc.brc.berkeley.edu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/ssh_certs/brc_cert
    ForwardAgent yes
    IdentitiesOnly yes

Host savio-compute
    LogLevel QUIET
    StrictHostKeyChecking no
    ProxyJump savio
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/ssh_certs/brc_cert
    HostName n0029.savio4
    User USER_NAME

Host savio-ln
    User USER_NAME
    HostName hpc.brc.berkeley.edu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/ssh_certs/brc_cert
```

The three entries serve different purposes:

- **`savio`** — General-purpose entry used as a proxy jump target.
- **`savio-compute`** — Points to a specific compute node (you will update `HostName` each session).
- **`savio-ln`** — Connects directly to the login node for quick administrative tasks.

Verify login-node access:

```bash
ssh savio-ln
```

### Step 3: Create a compute-node request script {#sec-get-compute}

::: {.callout-important}
Never run computationally intensive work on the login node. It is a shared resource and heavy usage affects all users.
:::

On the Savio login node, create a helper script:

```{.bash filename="~/scripts/get_compute.sh"}
#!/bin/bash
salloc -A co_carleton \
       -p savio4_htc \
       --qos=carleton_htc4_normal \
       -t 08:00:00 \
       -c 12 \
       --no-shell
```

Make it executable and alias it:

```bash
chmod u+x ~/scripts/get_compute.sh
```

Add to your `~/.bashrc` on Savio:

```bash
alias compute="~/scripts/get_compute.sh"
```

## Daily workflow {#sec-daily-workflow}

Once everything is configured, the daily connection process is:

### 1. Get a certificate

```bash
cert
# Enter username, PIN, MFA code
```

### 2. Request a compute node

```bash
ssh savio-ln
compute
```

SLURM will print the name of the node you are assigned (e.g., `n0050.savio4`). Copy this.

### 3. Update your SSH config

Paste the node name into the `HostName` field of the `savio-compute` entry in your **local** `~/.ssh/config`:

```bash
Host savio-compute
    ...
    HostName n0050.savio4   # <-- update this each session
    ...
```

### 4. Connect from your editor

Open VS Code or Positron, find the **Remote SSH** panel, and select **savio-compute**. The extension tunnels through the login node (via `ProxyJump savio`) and drops you onto the compute node.

You may be prompted for your PIN + MFA one more time — this is normal.

::: {.callout-tip}
Once connected, open the integrated terminal. You now have a full interactive session on a compute node with the resources you requested in `get_compute.sh`.
:::
